message.content:
Adversaries may exploit NTFS file attributes to conceal malicious data, evading detection mechanisms. NTFS partitions maintain a Master File Table (MFT) that records file attributes, including Extended Attributes (EA) and Alternate Data Streams (ADS). These attributes can store arbitrary data or complete files, allowing adversaries to hide malware within metadata rather than in visible files. This technique can bypass static indicator scanning tools and anti-virus software.

Notable examples include APT32 and Astaroth using ADS to hide payloads, BitPaymer copying itself to ADS, and Regin storing encrypted executables in Extended Attributes. Tools like esentutl and Expand facilitate reading, writing, and copying data into ADS. 

Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and analyzing NTFS file attributes for unusual activity, employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect hidden data, and using file integrity monitoring (FIM) to track changes in file attributes. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help prevent exploitation of this technique.
